text: The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions in the pelvic region, which are attributed to a prior hysterectomy and radiation therapy, with visible challenges due to dense fibrous tissue.
The surgeon observes a loop of small bowel adhered to the incision site from the patient's previous hysterectomy, noting the appearance of radiation damage in the surrounding tissue.

A small serosal tear is visible, which has been closed with a stitch located below the surgeon's left hand. The surgeon continues to proceed carefully, preparing the surgical field without yet visualizing the rectum or associated vasculature.

The surgeon prepares to start the low anterior resection (LAR), revealing significant radiation damage. The medial aspect of the mesentery is visibly fused, with the lateral aspects also fused, including the right side, which is completely adhered to the pelvic sidewall. These adhesions are characteristic of radiation changes.

The surgeon begins medial mobilization of the intraperitoneal rectum, which is fused to the right side. The mesentery is difficult to elevate due to the sigmoid colon being adhered to the left sidewall. The surgeon prepares to address this adhesion to facilitate elevation beneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery and the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).

The surgeon performs a left lateral dissection without mobilizing the splenic flexure, achieving a tension-free anastomosis in the pelvis. The procedure progresses without the use of ureteral catheters.
The surgeon carefully checks the ureter along the left pelvic sidewall, observing the area for signs of radiation-induced adhesions.

The surgeon observes the anterior fusion of the bladder flap to the intraperitoneal rectum and continues mobilizing the left side. A quick ureter check is performed, followed by packing a sponge to address minor oozing. The sigmoid colon is sufficiently mobilized, allowing the surgeon to proceed with medial dissection.

The surgeon enters the posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, revealing the right side of the TME plane. This maneuver exposes the left and right sides, facilitating dissection in the hostile pelvic environment before addressing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, focusing on improving rectal mobility. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is identified and cleaned, revealing significant fibrosis likely due to radiation in the lower abdomen. The surgeon prepares to ligate the IMA.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field, identifying the sponge placed earlier. A careful examination reveals the ureter, which is now clearly visible, ensuring it is not mistaken for the gonadal vessels.

The surgeon demonstrates mobilization efforts without needing to approach the splenic flexure. The focus is on the left anterior side, where the mesorectal fat is visibly adherent to the bladder, making it difficult to identify the typical 'holy plane' characterized by a white, crackly appearance.

The surgical field appears oozy and extremely edematous, likely due to previous radiation treatment. The surgeon anticipates encountering tethering on the back of the vagina, which is visible and attributed to scar tissue from a prior hysterectomy.

The surgeon uses suction in the left hand to maintain a clear view while dissecting deeper into the surgical field. The proper plane becomes more visible as the dissection progresses anteriorly, revealing an area of tissue adherence.

The surgeon switches to a vessel sealer for deeper dissection in the TME plane, taking broad bites to achieve hemostasis and divide tissue efficiently. The vessel sealer provides more hemostatic control compared to scissors, allowing for less meticulous but effective dissection while maintaining careful thermal spread.

The surgeon identifies the stricture at approximately seven centimeters and works to reach four to five centimeters, separating the adherence from the back of the vagina. The vagina is successfully separated, allowing the surgeon to resume scissor dissection above the levator floor.

The surgeon performs a tumor-specific mesorectal excision just above the levator floor, approximately three centimeters below the stricture. The anterior dissection is completed, exposing the anterior rectal wall. The surgeon uses scissors to amputate and continues to dissect around the mesorectum, focusing on the posterior part.

The surgeon uses four trocars in their normal positions and creates a separate stoma aperture due to the patient's high BMI and short stature. The mesentery is visibly manipulated and divided circumferentially, with efforts to achieve hemostasis and clean up excess fat tissue. The surgeon palpates from below to ensure proper space before performing a stapled anastomosis using an EEA stapler due to limited exposure in the deep pelvis.

The surgeon removes part of the meso to achieve a more complete total mesorectal excision (TME). The robot is undocked, and the specimen is extracted through the ileostomy aperture. An anvil is placed, and an anastomosis is created, followed by the formation of a diverting loop ileostomy. The surgical field is hemostatic, and no drain is left in place.
